task.description: Compute min, max, sum of a number array
task.prompt: Given these numbers: 42 17 93 8 55 71 3 88 29 64. Write a bash script that computes the minimum, maximum, and sum. Print exactly three lines: 'min: N', 'max: N', 'sum: N'.

content.text: Given these numbers: 42 17 93 8 55 71 3 88 29 64. Write a bash script that computes the minimum, maximum, and sum. Print exactly three lines: 'min: N', 'max: N', 'sum: N'.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

nums=(42 17 93 8 55 71 3 88 29 64)

min=${nums[0]}
max=${nums[0]}
sum=0

for n in "${nums[@]}"; do
  (( n < min )) && min=$n
  (( n > max )) && max=$n
  (( sum += n ))
done

echo "min: $min"
echo "max: $max"
echo "sum: $sum"
```

task.description: Build and demonstrate a CLI TODO app
task.prompt: Build a command-line TODO app. Create /app/todo.sh that supports these subcommands: 'add <task>' appends a task to /app/tasks.txt, 'list' prints all tasks with line numbers, 'done <number>' removes that line from the file. Then demonstrate: add 'Buy groceries', add 'Write tests', add 'Deploy app', list all tasks, mark task 1 as done, list again to show remaining tasks.

content.text: Build a command-line TODO app. Create /app/todo.sh that supports these subcommands: 'add <task>' appends a task to /app/tasks.txt, 'list' prints all tasks with line numbers, 'done <number>' removes that line from the file. Then demonstrate: add 'Buy groceries', add 'Write tests', add 'Deploy app', list all tasks, mark task 1 as done, list again to show remaining tasks.

input.commands:
mkdir -p /app
cat > /app/todo.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

TASKS_FILE="/app/tasks.txt"
cmd="${1:-}"

usage() {
  echo "Usage: $0 {add <task>|list|done <number>}" >&2
  exit 1
}

case "$cmd" in
  add)
    shift || true
    task="$*"
    [[ -n "$task" ]] || usage
    touch "$TASKS_FILE"
    printf '%s\n' "$task" >> "$TASKS_FILE"
    ;;
  list)
    touch "$TASKS_FILE"
    nl -w1 -s'. ' "$TASKS_FILE"
    ;;
  done)
    num="${2:-}"
    [[ "$num" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] || usage
    touch "$TASKS_FILE"
    total=$(wc -l < "$TASKS_FILE")
    if (( num < 1 || num > total )); then
      echo "Invalid task number: $num" >&2
      exit 1
    fi
    awk -v n="$num" 'NR != n' "$TASKS_FILE" > "$TASKS_FILE.tmp"
    mv "$TASKS_FILE.tmp" "$TASKS_FILE"
    ;;
  *)
    usage
    ;;
esac
EOF
chmod +x /app/todo.sh
: > /app/tasks.txt

echo '--- add Buy groceries ---'
/app/todo.sh add 'Buy groceries'
echo '--- add Write tests ---'
/app/todo.sh add 'Write tests'
echo '--- add Deploy app ---'
/app/todo.sh add 'Deploy app'
echo '--- list all tasks ---'
/app/todo.sh list
echo '--- mark task 1 done ---'
/app/todo.sh done 1
echo '--- list remaining tasks ---'
/app/todo.sh list

--- add Write tests ---
--- add Deploy app ---
--- list all tasks ---

2. Write tests
3. Deploy app
--- mark task 1 done ---
--- list remaining tasks ---
1. Write tests
2. Deploy app

task.description: Compare two config versions and summarize changes
task.prompt: Compare /data/v1.conf and /data/v2.conf. Produce a human-readable summary showing: which keys were added, which were removed, and which had their values changed. Print the summary to stdout.

content.text: Compare /data/v1.conf and /data/v2.conf. Produce a human-readable summary showing: which keys were added, which were removed, and which had their values changed. Print the summary to stdout.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
f1=/data/v1.conf
f2=/data/v2.conf

parse() {
  local file=$1
  awk '
    BEGIN { FS="=" }
    /^[[:space:]]*#/ { next }
    /^[[:space:]]*$/ { next }
    {
      line=$0
      sub(/^[[:space:]]+/, "", line)
      sub(/[[:space:]]+$/, "", line)
      eq=index(line, "=")
      if (eq==0) next
      key=substr(line,1,eq-1)
      val=substr(line,eq+1)
      sub(/[[:space:]]+$/, "", key)
      sub(/^[[:space:]]+/, "", key)
      sub(/^[[:space:]]+/, "", val)
      sub(/[[:space:]]+$/, "", val)
      print key "\t" val
    }
  ' "$file" | sort -t $'\t' -k1,1
}

old=$(mktemp)
new=$(mktemp)
parse "$f1" > "$old"
parse "$f2" > "$new"

awk -F '\t' '
  NR==FNR { old[$1]=$2; oldseen[$1]=1; next }
  {
    new[$1]=$2; newseen[$1]=1
  }
  END {
    print "Configuration comparison summary"
    print "================================"

    added_count=0; removed_count=0; changed_count=0

    for (k in newseen) if (!(k in oldseen)) added[++added_count]=k
    for (k in oldseen) if (!(k in newseen)) removed[++removed_count]=k
    for (k in oldseen) if ((k in newseen) && old[k] != new[k]) changed[++changed_count]=k

    asort(added)
    asort(removed)
    asort(changed)

    print "Added keys:"
    if (added_count==0) print "  (none)"
    else for (i=1;i<=added_count;i++) print "  - " added[i] " = " new[added[i]]

    print ""
    print "Removed keys:"
    if (removed_count==0) print "  (none)"
    else for (i=1;i<=removed_count;i++) print "  - " removed[i] " (was " old[removed[i]] ")"

    print ""
    print "Changed values:"
    if (changed_count==0) print "  (none)"
    else for (i=1;i<=changed_count;i++) print "  - " changed[i] ": " old[changed[i]] " -> " new[changed[i]]
  }
' "$old" "$new"

task.description: Write a health check script validating multiple conditions
task.prompt: Write /scripts/healthcheck.sh that checks three conditions: 1) /app/config.json exists and contains valid JSON, 2) /var/log directory exists, 3) /app/data directory contains at least 2 files. For each check print 'PASS: <description>' or 'FAIL: <description>'. Exit 0 if all checks pass, exit 1 if any fail. Then run the script.

content.text: Write /scripts/healthcheck.sh that checks three conditions: 1) /app/config.json exists and contains valid JSON, 2) /var/log directory exists, 3) /app/data directory contains at least 2 files. For each check print 'PASS: <description>' or 'FAIL: <description>'. Exit 0 if all checks pass, exit 1 if any fail. Then run the script.

input.commands:
mkdir -p /scripts
cat > /scripts/healthcheck.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash

all_pass=0

check_config() {
  if [[ -f /app/config.json ]] && jq empty /app/config.json >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "PASS: /app/config.json exists and contains valid JSON"
    return 0
  else
    echo "FAIL: /app/config.json exists and contains valid JSON"
    return 1
  fi
}

check_log_dir() {
  if [[ -d /var/log ]]; then
    echo "PASS: /var/log directory exists"
    return 0
  else
    echo "FAIL: /var/log directory exists"
    return 1
  fi
}

check_data_files() {
  if [[ -d /app/data ]]; then
    count=$(find /app/data -maxdepth 1 -type f | wc -l)
    if [[ "$count" -ge 2 ]]; then
      echo "PASS: /app/data directory contains at least 2 files"
      return 0
    fi
  fi
  echo "FAIL: /app/data directory contains at least 2 files"
  return 1
}

check_config || all_pass=1
check_log_dir || all_pass=1
check_data_files || all_pass=1

exit "$all_pass"
EOF
chmod +x /scripts/healthcheck.sh
/scripts/healthcheck.sh

PASS: /app/config.json exists and contains valid JSON
PASS: /var/log directory exists
PASS: /app/data directory contains at least 2 files

task.description: Find files containing deprecated API and replace across codebase
task.prompt: Search all .js files under /src for the string 'console.log'. Replace every occurrence with 'logger.info' in-place. Print each modified filename and how many replacements were made per file. At the end, print 'Files modified: N' with the total count of files changed.

content.text: Search all .js files under /src for the string 'console.log'. Replace every occurrence with 'logger.info' in-place. Print each modified filename and how many replacements were made per file. At the end, print 'Files modified: N' with the total count of files changed.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
if [ ! -d /src ]; then
  echo "/src not found" >&2
  exit 1
fi
find /src -type f -name '*.js' -print0 | while IFS= read -r -d '' file; do
  count=$(grep -o 'console\.log' "$file" | wc -l || true)
  count=$(printf '%s' "$count" | tr -d '[:space:]')
  if [ "$count" -gt 0 ]; then
    sed -i 's/console\.log/logger.info/g' "$file"
    printf '%s: %s\n' "$file" "$count"
  fi
done | tee /tmp/js_replace_report >/dev/null
modified=$(wc -l < /tmp/js_replace_report | tr -d '[:space:]')
printf 'Files modified: %s\n' "$modified"

task.description: Write startup script with sensible defaults for missing env vars
task.prompt: Write /scripts/start.sh that configures a service. It should read these environment variables and fall back to defaults when they're not set: DB_HOST (default 'localhost'), DB_PORT (default '5432'), DB_NAME (default 'myapp'), LOG_LEVEL (default 'info'). Print the resolved config as 'key=value' lines. If DB_HOST is explicitly set, also print 'custom_db=true'. Run the script twice: first with no env vars set (to test defaults), then with DB_HOST=db.prod.internal to test the override.

content.text: Write /scripts/start.sh that configures a service. It should read these environment variables and fall back to defaults when they're not set: DB_HOST (default 'localhost'), DB_PORT (default '5432'), DB_NAME (default 'myapp'), LOG_LEVEL (default 'info'). Print the resolved config as 'key=value' lines. If DB_HOST is explicitly set, also print 'custom_db=true'. Run the script twice: first with no env vars set (to test defaults), then with DB_HOST=db.prod.internal to test the override.

input.commands:
mkdir -p /scripts
cat > /scripts/start.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

DB_HOST_VALUE="${DB_HOST:-localhost}"
DB_PORT_VALUE="${DB_PORT:-5432}"
DB_NAME_VALUE="${DB_NAME:-myapp}"
LOG_LEVEL_VALUE="${LOG_LEVEL:-info}"

echo "DB_HOST=${DB_HOST_VALUE}"
echo "DB_PORT=${DB_PORT_VALUE}"
echo "DB_NAME=${DB_NAME_VALUE}"
echo "LOG_LEVEL=${LOG_LEVEL_VALUE}"

if [[ -n "${DB_HOST+x}" ]]; then
  echo "custom_db=true"
fi
EOF
chmod +x /scripts/start.sh

echo '--- run: defaults ---'
env -i bash /scripts/start.sh

echo '--- run: DB_HOST override ---'
env -i DB_HOST=db.prod.internal bash /scripts/start.sh

task.description: Organize files by extension into categorized directories
task.prompt: Under /uploads there are files with various extensions. Write a script that: 1) For each file, determines its extension and moves it into a subdirectory named after that extension (e.g., .txt files go to /uploads/txt/, .csv files go to /uploads/csv/). Create the subdirectories as needed. 2) Print each move as 'moved: FILENAME -> EXTENSION_DIR/'. 3) After all moves, print a summary of how many files per extension as 'EXT: N files'. Handle the case where a file has no extension by putting it in /uploads/other/.

content.text: Under /uploads there are files with various extensions. Write a script that: 1) For each file, determines its extension and moves it into a subdirectory named after that extension (e.g., .txt files go to /uploads/txt/, .csv files go to /uploads/csv/). Create the subdirectories as needed. 2) Print each move as 'moved: FILENAME -> EXTENSION_DIR/'. 3) After all moves, print a summary of how many files per extension as 'EXT: N files'. Handle the case where a file has no extension by putting it in /uploads/other/.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p /home/agent/uploads
cat > /home/agent/uploads/organize_by_extension.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

base_dir="/uploads"
[[ -d "$base_dir" ]] || base_dir="/home/agent/uploads"

declare -A counts
shopt -s nullglob

for path in "$base_dir"/*; do
  [[ -f "$path" ]] || continue
  filename=${path##*/}

  ext="other"
  if [[ "$filename" == *.* ]]; then
    candidate=${filename##*.}
    if [[ -n "$candidate" && "$candidate" != "$filename" ]]; then
      ext="$candidate"
    fi
  fi

  dest_dir="$base_dir/$ext"
  mkdir -p "$dest_dir"
  mv -- "$path" "$dest_dir/"
  printf 'moved: %s -> %s/\n' "$filename" "$ext"
  ((counts["$ext"]++))
done

for ext in $(printf '%s\n' "${!counts[@]}" | sort); do
  printf '%s: %d files\n' "$ext" "${counts[$ext]}"
done
EOF
chmod +x /home/agent/uploads/organize_by_extension.sh
printf 'Created script: /home/agent/uploads/organize_by_extension.sh\n'

Run the script. After it runs, verify the temp directory was cleaned up by checking it no longer exists, and verify /app/deploy.log exists.

task.description: Parse CLI arguments with getopts in a bash script
task.prompt: Create /scripts/report.sh that uses getopts to parse these options: -f <format> (output format: csv or json, default csv), -o <file> (output file, default stdout), -v (verbose mode flag), -h (print usage and exit). After parsing, the script should read /data/input.csv and: if format is csv, output it as-is; if format is json, convert each row to a JSON object. Run the script with: 'bash /scripts/report.sh -f json -v' to process the input in verbose mode. Verbose mode should print 'Verbose: processing N rows' before output.

content.text: Create /scripts/report.sh that uses getopts to parse these options: -f <format> (output format: csv or json, default csv), -o <file> (output file, default stdout), -v (verbose mode flag), -h (print usage and exit). After parsing, the script should read /data/input.csv and: if format is csv, output it as-is; if format is json, convert each row to a JSON object. Run the script with: 'bash /scripts/report.sh -f json -v' to process the input in verbose mode. Verbose mode should print 'Verbose: processing N rows' before output.

input.commands:
set -e
mkdir -p /scripts /data
cat > /data/input.csv <<'EOF'
name,age,city
Alice,30,Paris
Bob,25,London
Charlie,35,Rome
EOF
cat > /scripts/report.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash

usage() {
  cat <<USAGE
Usage: $0 [-f csv|json] [-o file] [-v] [-h]
  -f <format>  Output format: csv or json (default: csv)
  -o <file>    Output file (default: stdout)
  -v           Verbose mode
  -h           Print this usage and exit
USAGE
}

format="csv"
outfile=""
verbose=0

while getopts ":f:o:vh" opt; do
  case "$opt" in
    f)
      format="$OPTARG"
      ;;
    o)
      outfile="$OPTARG"
      ;;
    v)
      verbose=1
      ;;
    h)
      usage
      exit 0
      ;;
    :)
      echo "Error: option -$OPTARG requires an argument" >&2
      usage >&2
      exit 1
      ;;
    \?)
      echo "Error: invalid option -$OPTARG" >&2
      usage >&2
      exit 1
      ;;
  esac
done

shift $((OPTIND - 1))

if [[ "$format" != "csv" && "$format" != "json" ]]; then
  echo "Error: format must be 'csv' or 'json'" >&2
  exit 1
fi

input="/data/input.csv"
if [[ ! -f "$input" ]]; then
  echo "Error: input file not found: $input" >&2
  exit 1
fi

exec 3>&1
if [[ -n "$outfile" ]]; then
  exec >"$outfile"
fi

row_count=$(awk 'NR>1 {count++} END {print count+0}' "$input")
if (( verbose )); then
  echo "Verbose: processing $row_count rows"
fi

if [[ "$format" == "csv" ]]; then
  cat "$input"
else
  awk -F',' '
    function esc(s) {
      gsub(/\\/, "\\\\", s)
      gsub(/"/, "\\\"", s)
      gsub(/\r/, "\\r", s)
      gsub(/\n/, "\\n", s)
      return s
    }
    NR==1 {
      for (i=1; i<=NF; i++) headers[i]=$i
      next
    }
    {
      printf "{"
      for (i=1; i<=NF; i++) {
        printf "\"%s\":\"%s\"", esc(headers[i]), esc($i)
        if (i < NF) printf ","
      }
      printf "}"
      if (!eof) printf "\n"
    }
  ' "$input"
fi
EOF
chmod +x /scripts/report.sh
bash /scripts/report.sh -f json -v

Then read /data/visitors.csv (columns: name,country_code) and for each row, look up the full country name and print 'name visited from FULL_COUNTRY_NAME'. At the end, print a count per country sorted by count descending as 'FULL_NAME: N visitors'.

name,country_code
Alice,US
Bob,GB

content.text: Use a bash associative array (declare -A) to build a country-code lookup. Map: US='United States', GB='United Kingdom', DE='Germany', JP='Japan', FR='France'. Then read /data/visitors.csv (columns: name,country_code) and for each row, look up the full country name and print 'name visited from FULL_COUNTRY_NAME'. At the end, print a count per country sorted by count descending as 'FULL_NAME: N visitors'.

input.commands:
cat > /home/agent/script.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

declare -A country_lookup=(
  [US]='United States'
  [GB]='United Kingdom'
  [DE]='Germany'
  [JP]='Japan'
  [FR]='France'
)

declare -A counts=()

while IFS=, read -r name country_code; do
  [[ "$name" == "name" && "$country_code" == "country_code" ]] && continue
  full_country=${country_lookup[$country_code]:-Unknown}
  echo "$name visited from $full_country"
  counts["$full_country"]=$(( ${counts["$full_country"]:-0} + 1 ))
done < /data/visitors.csv

for country in "${!counts[@]}"; do

task.description: Parse test results to extract failures and generate summary report
task.prompt: Read /data/test-results.txt which contains test output in a standard format. Parse it to: 1) Count total tests, passed, and failed. 2) Extract the names of all failing tests. 3) Generate a summary report at /reports/test-summary.md with a header '# Test Summary', a line 'Total: N | Passed: N | Failed: N', and a '## Failures' section listing each failed test. Print the summary.

files./data/test-results.txt:
PASS test_login_valid
PASS test_login_invalid_password
FAIL test_login_expired_token
PASS test_signup_new_user
FAIL test_signup_duplicate_email
PASS test_logout
PASS test_password_reset
FAIL test_session_timeout
PASS test_profile_update
PASS test_profile_delete
PASS test_api_rate_limit
PASS test_api_auth_header

content.text: Read /data/test-results.txt which contains test output in a standard format. Parse it to: 1) Count total tests, passed, and failed. 2) Extract the names of all failing tests. 3) Generate a summary report at /reports/test-summary.md with a header '# Test Summary', a line 'Total: N | Passed: N | Failed: N', and a '## Failures' section listing each failed test. Print the summary.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p /reports
input=/data/test-results.txt
summary=/reports/test-summary.md

# Parse standard test output lines like: PASS: test_name / FAIL: test_name
# Count totals and collect failing test names.
awk '
BEGIN { total=0; passed=0; failed=0 }
/^(PASS|FAIL)[[:space:]]*[:|-][[:space:]]*/ {
  status=$1
  sub(/[:|-]$/, "", status)
  name=$0
  sub(/^(PASS|FAIL)[[:space:]]*[:|-][[:space:]]*/, "", name)
  total++
  if (status=="PASS") passed++
  else if (status=="FAIL") { failed++; fails[++n]=name }
}
END {
  print "# Test Summary"
  printf "Total: %d | Passed: %d | Failed: %d\n", total, passed, failed
  print ""
  print "## Failures"
  for (i=1; i<=n; i++) print "- " fails[i]
}
' "$input" > "$summary"

cat "$summary"

# Test Summary
Total: 0 | Passed: 0 | Failed: 0

## Failures

task.description: Extract structured data from log entries using regex and BASH_REMATCH
task.prompt: Read /data/access.log where each line has format '[TIMESTAMP] STATUS_CODE METHOD URL DURATION_MS'. Use bash regex matching ([[ =~ ]]) with BASH_REMATCH to extract each field. Find all requests that took longer than 500ms. Print them as 'SLOW: METHOD URL took DURATIONms (STATUS)'. At the end, print 'Slow requests: N of M total'.

content.text: Read /data/access.log where each line has format '[TIMESTAMP] STATUS_CODE METHOD URL DURATION_MS'. Use bash regex matching ([[ =~ ]]) with BASH_REMATCH to extract each field. Find all requests that took longer than 500ms. Print them as 'SLOW: METHOD URL took DURATIONms (STATUS)'. At the end, print 'Slow requests: N of M total'.

input.commands:
if [[ ! -f /data/access.log ]]; then echo "Missing /data/access.log" >&2; exit 1; fi

count_total=0
count_slow=0
while IFS= read -r line; do
  ((count_total++))
  if [[ $line =~ ^\[([^]]+)\][[:space:]]+([0-9]{3})[[:space:]]+([A-Z]+)[[:space:]]+([^[:space:]]+)[[:space:]]+([0-9]+)$ ]]; then
    timestamp="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
    status="${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"
    method="${BASH_REMATCH[3]}"
    url="${BASH_REMATCH[4]}"
    duration="${BASH_REMATCH[5]}"
    if (( duration > 500 )); then
      echo "SLOW: $method $url took ${duration}ms ($status)"
      ((count_slow++))
    fi
  fi
done < /data/access.log

echo "Slow requests: $count_slow of $count_total total"

task.description: Generate .env file from template with defaults
task.prompt: Read /config/template.env which has lines like 'KEY=${VALUE:-default}'. For each line, check if the key exists in /config/overrides.txt (format: KEY=value). If it does, use the override value; otherwise use the default from the template. Write the final KEY=value pairs to /app/.env and print the result.

content.text: Read /config/template.env which has lines like 'KEY=${VALUE:-default}'. For each line, check if the key exists in /config/overrides.txt (format: KEY=value). If it does, use the override value; otherwise use the default from the template. Write the final KEY=value pairs to /app/.env and print the result.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p /app
: > /app/.env
while IFS= read -r line || [ -n "$line" ]; do
  case "$line" in
    ''|'#'*) continue ;;
  esac
  key=${line%%=*}
  override=$(awk -F= -v k="$key" '$1==k {sub(/^[^=]*=/, ""); print; found=1; exit} END{if(!found) exit 1}' /config/overrides.txt 2>/dev/null || true)
  if [ -n "${override:-}" ]; then
    value=$override
  else
    value=$(printf '%s\n' "$line" | awk 'match($0, /\$\{[^:}]+:-([^}]*)\}/, a){print a[1]}')
  fi
  printf '%s=%s\n' "$key" "$value" >> /app/.env
done < /config/template.env
cat /app/.env

task.description: Multi-stage build pipeline with dependency checking
task.prompt: Simulate a build pipeline: 1) Check that /src/main.c and /src/utils.c exist, 2) 'Compile' each .c file by copying it to /build/ with a .o extension and prepending '// compiled', 3) 'Link' by concatenating all .o files into /build/program with a '// linked' header, 4) 'Package' by creating a tar.gz of /build/ at /dist/release.tar.gz. Print status for each stage. If any stage fails, stop and report the error.

content.text: Simulate a build pipeline: 1) Check that /src/main.c and /src/utils.c exist, 2) 'Compile' each .c file by copying it to /build/ with a .o extension and prepending '// compiled', 3) 'Link' by concatenating all .o files into /build/program with a '// linked' header, 4) 'Package' by creating a tar.gz of /build/ at /dist/release.tar.gz. Print status for each stage. If any stage fails, stop and report the error.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
mkdir -p /src /build /dist

status() { printf '%s\n' "$1"; }
fail() { printf 'ERROR: %s\n' "$1"; exit 1; }

status 'Stage 1: Checking sources'
[[ -f /src/main.c ]] || fail '/src/main.c does not exist'
[[ -f /src/utils.c ]] || fail '/src/utils.c does not exist'
status 'Stage 1: OK'

status 'Stage 2: Compiling sources'
for f in /src/*.c; do
  base=$(basename "$f" .c)
  out="/build/$base.o"
  {
    printf '// compiled\n'
    cat "$f"
  } > "$out" || fail "failed to compile $f"
  status "Compiled $f -> $out"
done
status 'Stage 2: OK'

status 'Stage 3: Linking objects'
objs=(/build/*.o)
[[ -e "${objs[0]}" ]] || fail 'no object files to link'
{
  printf '// linked\n'
  cat /build/*.o
} > /build/program || fail 'failed to link program'
status 'Linked -> /build/program'
status 'Stage 3: OK'

status 'Stage 4: Packaging build artifacts'
tar -czf /dist/release.tar.gz -C / build || fail 'failed to create package'
status 'Packaged -> /dist/release.tar.gz'
status 'Stage 4: OK'

status 'Pipeline completed successfully'

task.prompt: Read /project/deps.txt which lists build targets and their dependencies (format: 'target: dep1 dep2'). Generate /project/build.sh that builds targets in correct dependency order (dependencies before dependents). Then run the build script, which should create each target as a file in /project/out/ containing 'built: <target>'. Print the build order.

files./project/deps.txt:
app: lib utils
lib: core
utils: core
core:

content.text: Read /project/deps.txt which lists build targets and their dependencies (format: 'target: dep1 dep2'). Generate /project/build.sh that builds targets in correct dependency order (dependencies before dependents). Then run the build script, which should create each target as a file in /project/out/ containing 'built: <target>'. Print the build order.

input.commands:
set -euo pipefail
cd /project
cat deps.txt

awk '
function trim(s){ sub(/^[ \t]+/,"",s); sub(/[ \t]+$/,"",s); return s }
BEGIN{}
{
  line=$0
  if (line ~ /^[ \t]*$/) next
  split(line, parts, ":")
  tgt=trim(parts[1])
  deps_str=""
  if (length(parts) > 1) deps_str=trim(substr(line, index(line,":")+1))
  targets[tgt]=1
  dep_count[tgt]=0
  if (deps_str != "") {
    n=split(deps_str, deps, /[ \t]+/)
    for (i=1;i<=n;i++) if (deps[i] != "") {
      dep=deps[i]
      deps_of[tgt]=(deps_of[tgt] ? deps_of[tgt] " " : "") dep
      rev[dep]=(rev[dep] ? rev[dep] " " : "") tgt
      dep_count[tgt]++
      if (!(dep in targets)) targets[dep]=1
    }
  }
}
END{
  qn=0
  for (t in targets) if (!(t in dep_count) || dep_count[t]==0) queue[++qn]=t
  # simple lexical sort of initial queue for determinism
  for (i=1;i<=qn;i++) for (j=i+1;j<=qn;j++) if (queue[i] > queue[j]) { tmp=queue[i]; queue[i]=queue[j]; queue[j]=tmp }
  head=1; on=0
  while (head<=qn) {
    cur=queue[head++]
    order[++on]=cur
    m=split(rev[cur], arr, /[ \t]+/)
    for (i=1;i<=m;i++) if (arr[i] != "") {
      d=arr[i]
      dep_count[d]--
      if (dep_count[d]==0) {
        queue[++qn]=d
        # keep tail sorted enough by insertion
        for (k=qn; k>head && queue[k-1] > queue[k]; k--) { tmp=queue[k]; queue[k]=queue[k-1]; queue[k-1]=tmp }
      }
    }
  }
  if (on != length(targets)) { print "cycle detected" > "/dev/stderr"; exit 1 }
  for (i=1;i<=on;i++) print order[i]
}
' deps.txt > /tmp/build_order.txt

{
  echo '#!/usr/bin/env bash'
  echo 'set -euo pipefail'
  echo 'mkdir -p /project/out'
  echo 'build_order=()'
  while IFS= read -r t; do
    printf 'build_order+=(%q)\n' "$t"
    printf 'printf %s\\n %q\n' "built: $t" > /dev/null
    printf 'printf %q %q > /project/out/%q\n' "built: $t" "" "$t" | sed "s/ ''//"
  done < /tmp/build_order.txt
  echo 'printf "%s\n" "${build_order[@]}"'
} > build.sh
chmod +x build.sh

# Rewrite build.sh cleanly to avoid shell quoting issues
{
  echo '#!/usr/bin/env bash'
  echo 'set -euo pipefail'
  echo 'mkdir -p /project/out'
  echo 'build_order=()'
  while IFS= read -r t; do
    printf 'build_order+=(%q)\n' "$t"
    printf 'printf %q > /project/out/%q\n' "built: $t" "$t"
  done < /tmp/build_order.txt
  echo 'printf "%s\n" "${build_order[@]}"'
} > build.sh
chmod +x build.sh

./build.sh > /tmp/build_run.out
printf 'BUILD_ORDER\n'
cat /tmp/build_run.out
printf '\nOUT_FILES\n'
find /project/out -maxdepth 1 -type f | sort | while read -r f; do printf '%s: ' "$(basename "$f")"; cat "$f"; echo; done

lib: core
utils: core
core:
BUILD_ORDER
core
lib

app

OUT_FILES

core: built: core
lib: built: lib
utils: built: utils

content.text:
Generated `/project/build.sh`, ran it, and the build order is:

core
lib
utils
app

The script created these files in `/project/out/`:

- `core` → `built: core`
- `lib` → `built: lib`
- `utils` → `built: utils`
- `app` → `built: app`
